aliases: Spicy Korean grilled pork with rice
Korean BBQ pork with steamed rice

english: Spicy Korean grilled pork with rice
Korean BBQ pork with steamed rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bulgogi entry; no specific pork bulgogi with rice composite entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork meat dish, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for marinated grilled pork bulgogi composite with rice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Korean pork bulgogi with rice as a composite dish. A0CKM (pork meat dish, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Korean pork bulgogi with rice composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and 150 g of marinated grilled pork bulgogi, as typically plated in a Korean restaurant or hawker setting.

nutrient_source.title: Korean Pork Bulgogi (with Rice) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Korean pork bulgogi with rice (CR ID: F05100069). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this primary source. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork loin, cooked (NDB 10062) and cooked white rice (NDB 20444) in proportion to dish composition, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean pork bulgogi with rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated from the GI of white rice (Jasmine/short-grain cooked white rice, GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database), which dominates the carbohydrate fraction of this dish (approximately 18.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from white rice). The pork and marinade components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (72 × 18.39 g × 3.5 serving factor) / 100 = 47.3 per 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 237 mg per 100 g from soy-sauce-based marinade; estimated 830 mg sodium per standard 350 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g and request reduced soy sauce in the marinade, as a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 830 mg sodium — one-third of the 2,300 mg daily limit — and the dish lacks sufficient potassium or dietary fibre to offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice dominates carbohydrate content at 18.4 g per 100 g with only 0.71 g dietary fibre, producing a high glycaemic load of approximately 47 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish in its standard form; substituting white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, reducing the rice portion to 100 g, and increasing non-starchy vegetable sides will substantially lower the glycaemic load and post-meal glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g) from pork, combined with 237 mg sodium per 100 g and refined carbohydrate from white rice, presents a composite cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in reduced portions, prioritising leaner pork cuts, requesting low-sodium soy sauce in the marinade, and pairing with fibre-rich vegetables to mitigate the combined saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; refined white rice further drives glycaemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of pork bulgogi and consider substituting pork with chicken breast or tofu bulgogi, replacing white rice with brown rice, and avoiding additional high-sodium condiments such as kimchi or dipping sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Korean Pork Bulgogi with Rice is a popular Korean-origin dish consisting of thinly sliced pork marinated in a sweet-savoury sauce — typically combining soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, sugar, and green onion — then grilled or pan-fried until caramelised, and served alongside a portion of steamed white rice. The dish delivers a balance of umami, sweetness, and slight smokiness from the caramelised marinade, with tender, juicy pork contrasting the soft, sticky texture of the rice. Per 100 g, the dish provides 160 kcal, 7.95 g protein, 6.07 g fat, and 18.39 g carbohydrate, with 237 mg sodium reflecting the soy-sauce-heavy marinade. The high-GI white rice (GI ~72) dominates the carbohydrate fraction, producing a high glycaemic load per standard serving (~47 GL per 350 g plate), making this dish a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The moderate saturated fat from pork and the elevated sodium from soy sauce also warrant caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Culturally, pork bulgogi is a staple of Korean home cooking and restaurant menus, widely available in Singapore's Korean dining establishments and food courts. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or soy allergens.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F05100069, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy proportional composition. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, which is a well-established and defensible analogous estimate.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Korean pork bulgogi with rice dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F05100069) or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite entry as of 2026. 2. Saturated fat (1.8 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy proportional composition of cooked pork loin and cooked white rice at approximate dish ratios (43% pork, 57% rice by weight), and is documented as an adapted value. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Korean pork bulgogi with rice as a composite dish was found in approved sources. 4. The dish is not halal-certified as a category default (pork); individual restaurant certifications may vary. 5. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for this composite dish.
